A full scale model was built and water performance test data were obtained in early 1955.
The model was installed in a test loop built of 6 in. pipe with head and flow measuring instrumentation and a throttling valve.
The pump was driven by 15-hp variable-speed, d-c motor.
pump was obscured by a flat plate located 1 in. below and parallel to the pump suction.
A second test was performed with the plate removed and replaced by an 8 in. pipe connected directly to the pump suction.
Since the flat plate was very difficult to install, the 8 in. pipe suction was used for all subsequent testing.

text: The helium concentration in the argon buffer gas used in the experiment reported by Stair was $10^{-4}$ , hence the actual attenuation in concentration between the tank and the seal may have been greater.

In the ART, as well as in the above mentioned test, 500 liters per day of buffer gas flows down through the clearance around the pump shaft into the expansion tank and 50 liters per day will be bled directly from the oil catch basin.
The attenuation of $10^{4}$ represents the back transfer of helium against the 500 liters per day of argon.
This back leakage was determined from analysis of the 50 liters per day bled from the catch basin.
Assuming an attenuation of $10^{4}$ and a purge rate of 3000 in. $^{3}$ /day (50 liters per day) the value of $S_{b}$ and $\lambda_{p}$ may be calculated as follows:

\lambda_ {4}\right)} \frac {\lambda_ {3}}{\left(\lambda_ {1} - \lambda_ {3}\right)} \frac {\lambda_ {2}}{\left(\lambda_ {1} - \lambda_ {2}\right)} \frac {N _ {1} ^ {T}}{\left(\lambda_ {1} + \lambda_ {p}\right)} \\ \end{array}

In the calculations it is assumed that the $3/8$ in. thick steel wall of the reservoir will give no attenuation for gamma energies above 0.03 Mev, an attenuation of 10 in the energy range of 0.01 to 0.03 Mev and 75 for energies below 0.01 Mev.
The data for yields, energies, and percent gammas are taken from Blomeke (16).
The data for dosages are taken from Rockwell (17).
The results of the calculations are given in Table F.1.
Columns 6 and 7 give the $\beta$ and $\gamma$ energy release rates in the fuel expansion tank.
These are equal to 15.7 and 0.1 Kw, respectively.
Column 9 tabulates the disintegration per sec of each nuclide and Column 10 converts the $\beta$ energy release of these
Columns 9 and 10 refer to the activity and energy release of only one of the two oil systems.
Column ll gives the $\gamma$ dose at a distance of 1 ft from either of the two fuel pump oil reservoirs.
This $\gamma$ dose rate is about $30\mathrm{mr / hr}$ at a distance of 1 ft and is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from these reservoirs.
The approximate volume of oil in each pump lubricant system is 30 gal.
If the total $\beta$ emitters in the oil are assumed equally dispersed throughout the system, the dose to the oil is found to be about 20 rads/hr.

This dose for 500 hr of operation at 60 Mw after equilibrium is reached amounts to $10^{4}$ rads, which is insignificant compared to the $10^{8}$ rads which the oil is capable of absorbing without serious damage.
Xe AND DAUGHTER PRODUCTS IN EXPANSION TANK AND OIL SYSTEM FOR A HELIUM FLOW RATE OF 1000 LITERS/DAY
NuclideDecay Constant AAve.
The total fuel flow through the expansion tank including the leakage around the island is about 23 gpm or $1450~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ .
The helium flow rate is treated as a parameter and is varied from 1000 to 5000 liters/day at standard temperature and pressure.
The solubility (18) of xenon in fuel is shown in Fig.
A total pressure of 2 atm and a temperature $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ were used to determine the partial pressure of xenon.
The partial pressures at helium flow rates of from 1000 to 5000 standard liters per day are given in Table G.l.
(2) gives the xenon concentration in the fuel.
Figure G.3 gives the total xenon concentration in the fuel for helium flow rates of 1000 and 5000 standard liters per day.
G.3 is the $\mathrm{Xe}^{135}$ concentration in the fuel.
This was obtained by taking $14\%$ of the total xenon (the percent shown in Table G.l is 13.63).
For a fuel with 5 mole % of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ the $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ concentration is about $8.2 \times 10^{20}$ atoms/cm $^3$ .
The ratio of the xenon-to-fuel cross section $\sigma^{\mathrm{x e}} / \sigma^{\mathrm{F}}$ for a Maxwellian distribution at ART thermal is $4.9 \times 10^{3}$ .
As approximately $22\%$ of the xenon cross section is scattering, the value of $\sigma^{\mathrm{x e}} / \sigma^{\mathrm{F}}$ is multiplied by 0.78.
In the ART about $40\%$ of the neutrons reach thermal energy.
G.4 shows the poisoning based on the total neutron density of the reactor and is assumed to be $40\%$ of that for the thermal neutrons.
The effect of the poisoning may be more easily understood by comparison to the temperature coefficient, which for the ART is expected to be about $2 \times 10^{-5} / {}^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Thus, a poisoning of $1 \times 10^{-3}$ is equivalent to a temperature change of $50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
